Impaired peripheral disposal leaves more glucose in circulation.

Absent insulin signaling prevents adequate systemic glucose utilization.

Excess ketone production can culminate in acute diabetic ketoacidosis.
Inadequate insulin availability relative to demand sustains elevated blood glucose.

Endothelial injury promotes inflammatory activation in vessel walls.

Endothelial dysfunction in large vessels accelerates atherosclerotic disease.
Persistent vascular inflammation exacerbates renal small-vessel injury.
Persistent vascular inflammation exacerbates retinal small-vessel injury.
Persistent vascular inflammation exacerbates neural small-vessel injury.
Vascular inflammation accelerates atherosclerotic plaque development.

nodes.description: Near-complete deficiency of endogenous insulin severely impairs metabolic glucose regulation.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetes: Type 1 Diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes is defined as a state of hyperglycemia due to insulin deficiency caused by autoimmune pancreatic beta-cell destruction.

nodes.description: Persistent urinary albumin excretion reflecting diabetic glomerular and tubular injury.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetic Kidney Disease: From Pathophysiology To Treatment Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Changes in glomerular hemodynamics, glomerular and tubular hypertrophy, hyperfiltration, overactivation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS), podocyte injury, inflammation, oxidative stress, renal hypoxia, mitochondrial injury, and epigenetic changes are the main mechanisms leading to albuminuria, glomerulosclerosis and fibrosis.

nodes.description: Atherosclerotic vascular disease increases arterial thrombus formation and ischemic end-organ injury risk.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Hyperglycemia-associated vascular injury, oxidative stress, inflammation and altered hemodynamic balance may initiate atherosclerosis development and formation of arterial thrombus [247].

nodes.description: HLA class II (HLA-DQ2/HLA-DQ8) and non-HLA susceptibility variants increase risk of islet-directed autoimmunity in type 1 diabetes pathways.

evidence.reference_title: Genetics of type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: The HLA-DQ genes are the primary susceptibility genes within this region, although other genes may also contribute. The IDDM2 locus maps to a variable number of tandem repeats in the insulin gene region on chromosome 11.

evidence.reference_title: Type 1 diabetes-associated HLA-DQ8 transdimer accommodates a unique peptide repertoire.
evidence.snippet: HLA-DQ2 and HLA-DQ8 are strongly predisposing haplotypes for type 1 diabetes (T1D).

evidence.reference_title: Monogenic Diabetes and Integrated Stress Response Genes Display Altered Gene Expression in Type 1 Diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Two dozen monogenic diabetes genes showed altered expression in human pancreata from individuals with T1D versus unaffected control subjects.

nodes.description: Immune-mediated loss of insulin-producing pancreatic beta cells drives insulin-deficient hyperglycemia in type 1 diabetes pathways.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetes: Type 1 Diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Type 1 diabetes is defined as a state of hyperglycemia due to insulin deficiency caused by autoimmune pancreatic beta-cell destruction.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus: a 90-year perspective.
evidence.snippet: demonstrating an immune-mediated loss of insulin-secreting β-cells in subjects with 'insulin-dependent' diabetes.

nodes.description: Cerebral vascular disease burden with elevated ischemic stroke risk in diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: T2DM leads to a two- to four-fold increase in the mortality rate of adults from heart disease and stroke and is associated with both micro- and macro-vascular complications, the latter consisting of accelerated atherosclerosis leading to severe peripheral vascular disease, premature coronary artery disease (CAD) and increased risk of cerebrovascular diseases [178,179,180].

nodes.description: Sustained elevation of blood glucose establishes the shared biochemical driver of long-term diabetes tissue injury.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: According to the World Health Organization (WHO) diabetes mellitus is a chronic, metabolic disease characterized by elevated levels of blood glucose, which leads over time to damage to the heart, vasculature, eyes, kidneys and nerves.

nodes.description: Progressive reduction in kidney function due to cumulative diabetic renal injury.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetic Kidney Disease: From Pathophysiology To Treatment Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: Diabetes mellitus (DM) is the most prevalent cause of CKD and end-stage kidney disease (ESKD), requiring renal replacement therapy (RRT).

evidence.reference_title: Diabetes mellitus with a duration of 26 years combined with IgA nephropathy: A case report and literature review.
evidence.snippet: Renal biopsy might aid in the definitive diagnosis of DKD, NDKD, and NDKD combined with DKD.

nodes.description: Atherosclerotic coronary artery narrowing that increases myocardial ischemic risk in diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetes Mellitus and Cardiovascular Disease: Exploring Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, and Treatment Strategies.
evidence.snippet: The predominant form of CVD among diabetic patients is coronary artery disease (CAD), accounting for 29.4% of cases.

nodes.description: Glomerular structural injury, including podocyte and filtration barrier damage, contributes to progressive diabetic nephropathy.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetic Kidney Disease: From Pathophysiology To Treatment Perspectives.

nodes.description: Acute life-threatening decompensation from insulin deficiency and ketone excess.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis: Evaluation and Treatment.
evidence.snippet: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) is a life-threatening complication of type 1 and type 2 diabetes resulting from an absolute or relative insulin deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Sintilimab-related diabetes mellitus and psoriasis: A case report and literature review.
evidence.snippet: Regrettably, the patient's personal decision to discontinue medication for a single day led to the emergence of acute ketoacidosis, coupled with a recurrence of psoriasis vulgaris.

nodes.description: Established diabetic kidney disease reflects cumulative glomerular, tubular, inflammatory, and fibrotic renal injury.

nodes.description: Structural and functional beta-cell damage can emerge before clinically apparent dysglycemia.

evidence.snippet: Additionally, significant damage to beta cells may occur even before dysglycemia develops.

nodes.description: Diabetic vascular beds lose balanced endothelial control of vascular tone and structure.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Endothelium plays an important role in the regulation of vascular tone and structure through a balanced release of endothelial-derived relaxing and contracting factors. This balance is altered in T2DM leading to alteration of the physicochemical properties of the vascular wall via endothelial dysfunction, oxidative stress, platelet hyperreactivity, and inflammation [240,241].

nodes.description: Chronic hyperglycemia increases advanced glycation end products and RAGE signaling, propagating vascular injury responses.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: The increase in O2− production activates the five major pathways involved in the pathogenesis of diabetes complications: enhancement of the polyol pathway, increased formation of advanced glycation end products (AGEs), increased expression of AGEs receptor and its activating ligands, activation of protein kinase C (PKC) isoforms, and overactivity of the hexosamine pathway [55,56,57].

nodes.description: Hyperglycemia-induced increases in reactive oxygen species amplify downstream vascular injury pathways.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanism of Generation of Oxidative Stress and Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: How Are They Interlinked?
evidence.snippet: Oxidative stress has been considered as a major hallmark for the pathogenesis and development of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM)

evidence.reference_title: Staging presymptomatic type 1 diabetes: a scientific statement of JDRF, the Endocrine Society, and the American Diabetes Association.
evidence.snippet: The highest non-HLA genetic contribution arises from the INS, PTPN22, CTLA4, and IL2RA genes, with the latter three genes also contributing to susceptibility to other autoimmune diseases (17).

nodes.description: Insulin resistance increases hepatic glucose production and raises fasting and postprandial glucose load.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: On the other hand, IR contributes to increased glucose production in the liver and decreased glucose uptake both in the muscle, liver and adipose tissue.

nodes.description: Severe insulin deficiency drives adipose lipolysis and hepatic ketone body production, predisposing to ketoacidosis.

evidence.reference_title: Glucose and ketone body kinetics in diabetic ketoacidosis.
evidence.snippet: Insulin deficiency results in increased rates of lipolysis and provides increased substrate (free fatty acids) for ketogenesis.

nodes.description: Impaired incretin signaling reduces glucose-stimulated insulin secretory responses, especially through blunted GIP pathway activity.

evidence.reference_title: GLP1-GIP receptor co-agonists: a promising evolution in the treatment of type 2 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: Dual glucagon like peptide 1 (GLP1) and glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP) receptor agonists are among the new pharmacological strategies recently developed to address this challenge.

nodes.description: Type I and type II interferon responses increase beta-cell immunogenicity, promoting progression to immune-mediated beta-cell destruction.

biological_processes: antigen processing and presentation of peptide antigen via MHC class I

evidence.reference_title: Interferons are key cytokines acting on pancreatic islets in type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: IFN-α and IFN-γ had a greater impact on the beta cell transcriptome when compared with IL-1β and TNF-α.

evidence.reference_title: Interferons are key cytokines acting on pancreatic islets in type 1 diabetes.
evidence.snippet: The IFN-induced gene signatures have a strong correlation with those observed in beta cells from individuals with type 1 diabetes, and the level of expression of specific IFN-stimulated genes is positively correlated with proteins present in islets of these individuals, regulating beta cell responses to 'danger signals' such as viral infections.

evidence.reference_title: Diabetes Mellitus and Cardiovascular Disease: Exploring Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, and Treatment Strategies.

nodes.description: Early mitochondrial dysfunction and pathological reactive oxygen species signaling across metabolic tissues can accelerate diabetes progression.
nodes.id: Mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress in metabolic tissues

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Oxidative Stress, and Inter-Organ Miscommunications in T2D Progression.
evidence.snippet: Regardless of the primary event (i.e., IR vs. β-cell dysfunction) that contributes to dysglycemia, significant early-onset oxidative damage and mitochondrial dysfunction in multiple metabolic tissues may be a driver of T2D onset and progression.

nodes.description: Chronic diabetic injury of neural microvessels contributes to ischemic and metabolic stress in peripheral nerves.

nodes.description: Defective insulin secretion by pancreatic beta cells reduces compensatory endocrine capacity and worsens dysglycemia progression.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.
evidence.snippet: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM), one of the most common metabolic disorders, is caused by a combination of two primary factors: defective insulin secretion by pancreatic β-cells and the inability of insulin-sensitive tissues to respond appropriately to insulin.

evidence.reference_title: Pancreatic β-cell dysfunction in type 2 diabetes: Implications of inflammation and oxidative stress.
evidence.snippet: Insulin resistance and pancreatic β-cell dysfunction are major pathological mechanisms implicated in the development and progression of type 2 diabetes (T2D).

evidence.reference_title: Diabetes Mellitus and Cardiovascular Disease: Exploring Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, and Treatment Strategies.
evidence.snippet: The pathophysiology of DM is complex, involving insulin resistance, β-cell dysfunction, and associated cardiovascular complications including diabetic cardiomyopathy (DCM) and cardiovascular autonomic neuropathy (CAN).

nodes.description: Some diabetes cases discussed within evolving T5DM literature are framed as pancreatogenic fibro-inflammatory disease with loss of key endocrine hormones, including insulin and glucagon.

evidence.reference_title: Type 5 diabetes mellitus: redefining pancreatogenic diabetes through molecular, imaging, and AI-driven evidence.
evidence.snippet: KEY FINDINGS: T5DM involves loss of insulin and glucagon alongside exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, malnutrition, and significant glycaemic variability.

nodes.description: Liver, skeletal muscle, and adipose tissue develop impaired biologic responses to insulin stimulation.
nodes.id: Peripheral insulin resistance in insulin-sensitive tissues

evidence.reference_title: Insulin Resistance.
evidence.snippet: All tissues with insulin receptors can become insulin resistant, but the tissues that primarily drive insulin resistance are the liver, skeletal muscle, and adipose tissue.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of type 2 diabetes mellitus in children and adolescents: treatment implications.
evidence.snippet: The pathophysiology, which involves both an insulin secretion defect and resistance to insulin, needs further clarification in pediatric studies.

evidence.reference_title: Pathophysiology of type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus: a 90-year perspective.
evidence.snippet: showed a reduced peripheral effect of insulin in subjects with 'non-insulin-dependent' diabetes (insulin resistance).

nodes.description: Prediabetes reflects a metabolic risk state that precedes overt diabetes and is associated with accelerated progression toward youth-onset type 2 diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: The Natural History of Prediabetes and Cardiovascular Disease in the Pediatric Population.
evidence.snippet: Individuals with prediabetes are at a higher risk of developing youth-onset type 2 diabetes, which is considered a more aggressive form of the disease.

nodes.description: Insulin resistance lowers glucose uptake in peripheral tissues, especially skeletal muscle and adipose compartments.
